title: Ignoring Files with .gitignore
intro: Not every file belongs in Git. Secret API keys, giant dependency folders, and system files should be ignored. Think of it like a bouncer at a club -- .gitignore decides which files get in and which ones stay outside. The .gitignore file tells Git which files to pretend don't exist.

teach.title: .gitignore = The Bouncer at the Door
teach.body: Imagine a bouncer at a club with a list of people who aren't allowed in. The `.gitignore` file is that list -- it tells Git which files to completely ignore. Those files won't show up in `git status`, won't get staged, and won't get committed. They're invisible to Git.

teach.title: Why Ignore Files?
teach.body:
Some files should **never** be committed:

- **Secret files** (`.env`) -- Contain API keys and passwords. Committing them is a security disaster.
- **Dependencies** (`node_modules/`) -- Can contain thousands of files and are always regenerated from `package.json`.
- **OS files** (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`) -- System junk that clutters up your repo.

teach.title: How .gitignore Works
teach.code:
# .gitignore file contents:
.env              # Ignore this exact file
node_modules/     # Ignore this entire folder
*.log             # Ignore all files ending in .log
.DS_Store         # Ignore macOS system files
teach.annotation: Each line is a pattern. You can use `*` as a wildcard and `/` for folders. Lines starting with `#` are comments.

teach.title: .gitignore = A Spam Filter for Your Repo
teach.body: Think of `.gitignore` like the spam filter on your email. Without it, your inbox gets flooded with junk -- newsletters, scams, ads. With it, only the important stuff gets through. Your `.gitignore` filters out the junk files (system files, secrets, dependencies) so your repository only contains what actually matters.

teach.title: Commands You'll Use
teach.code:
echo ".env" > .gitignore          # Create .gitignore with one rule
echo "node_modules/" >> .gitignore # Append another rule
git status                         # Verify ignored files are hidden
teach.annotation: Use `>` to create/overwrite and `>>` to append. Always run `git status` after to confirm it worked.

teach.body: Accidentally committing API keys to GitHub has cost companies millions of dollars. Knowing `.gitignore` protects your projects and your reputation -- it's one of the first things senior devs check when reviewing your code.
The `.gitignore` file tells Git which files to skip -- always ignore secrets, dependencies, and OS files from the very start of a project.

steps.instruction: Run git status to see all the files Git is tracking. Notice that .env (which contains secret keys) and node_modules/ (a huge dependency folder) are listed.
steps.output:
On branch main

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)

	.DS_Store
	.env
	app.js
	config.js
	node_modules/
	package.json

nothing added to commit (use "git add" to track)
steps.hint: Type 'git status' to see the current state of your working directory.

steps.instruction: Create a .gitignore file that tells Git to ignore the .env file. This file contains secret API keys that should never be committed.
steps.hint: Use 'echo' to write text to a file: echo ".env" > .gitignore

steps.instruction: Now add node_modules/ to the .gitignore as well. This folder can contain thousands of files and is always recreated from package.json.
steps.hint: Use '>>' (double arrow) to append to the file instead of overwriting it: echo "node_modules/" >> .gitignore

steps.instruction: Add .DS_Store to .gitignore too. These are macOS system files that clutter up repositories.
steps.hint: Append .DS_Store to .gitignore using >> just like the last step.

steps.instruction: Now run git status again. The ignored files should no longer appear.
steps.output:
On branch main

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)

	.gitignore
	app.js
	config.js
	package.json

nothing added to commit (use "git add" to track)
steps.hint: Type 'git status' to verify the ignored files are hidden.

exercise.completionMessage: The .env, node_modules/, and .DS_Store files are now invisible to Git. Your secrets stay secret, your repository stays small, and your teammates won't see your system files. Always set up .gitignore at the start of a project!
